stem: A hospital uses a PSA blood test to screen for prostate cancer. The test has a sensitivity of 76% and a specificity of 91%. The prevalence of prostate cancer in the patient population is estimated to be between 5.8% and 17.4%, with uncertainty due to regional variation and demographic differences in the hospital's catchment area. A patient receives a positive test result. What is the probability that the patient actually has prostate cancer? Provide your answer as a probability with a confidence interval reflecting the uncertainty in prevalence.

ground_truth.answer: P(prostate cancer | positive test) ranges from 0.342 to 0.64 as prevalence varies from 5.8% to 17.4%. Point estimate at 11.6% prevalence: 0.526.

solution_steps: Apply Bayes' theorem: P(D|+) = P(+|D)×P(D) / [P(+|D)×P(D) + P(+|¬D)×P(¬D)], where P(+|¬D) = 1-specificity = 0.09.
The confidence interval [0.342, 0.64] reflects the range of posterior probabilities over plausible prevalence values.
common_failure_modes: Confusing sensitivity (76%) with positive predictive value
Providing only a point estimate without acknowledging the prevalence uncertainty
Ignoring the base rate (prevalence) and using the test sensitivity as the answer
Not recognizing the strong base rate effect when prevalence is low
